edge: Column Generation (CG) is an iterative algorithm for solving linear programs (LPs) with an extremely large number of variables (columns).
Two canonical applications of Column Generation are the Cutting Stock Problem (CSP) and Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (VRPTW).

edge: In VRPTW, for example, each binary variable represents the decision to include or exclude a route, of which there are exponentially many.
We propose RLCG, the ﬁrst Reinforcement Learning (RL) approach for CG.
RLCG is the ﬁrst Reinforcement Learning (RL) approach for CG.
We treat CG as a sequential decision-making problem, using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to represent the variable-constraint structure in the LP of interest in RLCG.
We perform an extensive set of experiments using the publicly available BPPLIB benchmark for CSP.
We perform an extensive set of experiments using the publicly available Solomon benchmark for VRPTW.
RLCG converges faster and reduces the number of CG iterations by 22.4% for CSP and 40.9% for VRPTW on average compared to a commonly used greedy policy.

CSP is a general resource allocation problem where the objective is to subdivide a given quantity of a resource into a number of predetermined allocations to meet certain demands so that the total usage of the resources is minimized
CG is used to solve the LP relaxation of CSP iteratively without explicitly enumerating all possible patterns

Below, we provide an overview of the column generation algorithm, noting that our method will intervene in Step 4 to make a potentially non-greedy column selection decision. At a high level, our RL-aided column selection strategy, RLCG, works as follows.

At a high level, our RL-aided column selection strategy, RLCG, works as follows. The RL agent is fused within the CG loop and actively selects the column to be added to the next iteration using the information extracted from the current RMP and SP.

We use the well-known Solomon benchmark [Solomon, 1987]. This dataset contains six different problem types (C1, C2, R1, R2, RC1, RC2)
original Solomon instance can be used to generate smaller instances by considering only the first n < 50 customers
instances from types C1, R1, RC1 are used for training and 240 training instances are generated by considering the first n customers
Curriculum Design involves sequencing instances in order of difficulty: C1, R1, then RC1
clustered instances enable compact routes for neighboring customers
random instances require more complex routes compared to clustered instances
mixed RC1 instances require reasoning about both types of customers simultaneously
